perturbation.original_text: 1. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND EXCLUDED INFORMATION 1.1. “Confidential Information” shall mean information that is revealed by or obtained through a party (whether in writing, orally or by any other means) (a “Disclosing Party”) to the other (a “Receiving Party”) including, without limitation, (i) all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information including, for example, pricing, strategies, studies, assessments, plans, compilations, program devices, formulae, designs, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes and any information directly relating to the integrity or security of computer networks or systems; (ii) information traditionally recognized as trade secrets; (iii) all data and information about a party’s customers (current, former or prospective) and Employees and Advisors (as such terms are defined below); (iv) the terms and conditions of this Agreement (including, without limitation, the Disclosure Purpose); and (v) all copies of any of the foregoing or any analyses, studies or reports that contain, are based on, or reflect any of the foregoing. 1.2. The confidentiality obligations set forth under this Agreement do not extend to Excluded Information. For the purposes of this Agreement, “Excluded Information” means information that the Receiving Party can demonstrate (i) was known to the Receiving Party without restriction prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (ii) became publicly available through no act or omission of the Receiving Party; (iii) is received from a third party without breach of any obligation owed to the Disclosing Party; or (iv) was developed at any time by the Receiving Party independent of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND EXCLUDED INFORMATION 1.1. “Confidential Information” shall mean information that is revealed by or obtained through a party (whether in writing, orally or by any other means) (a “Disclosing Party”) to the other (a “Receiving Party”) including, without limitation, (i) all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information including, for example, pricing, strategies, studies, assessments, plans, compilations, program devices, formulae, designs, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes and any information directly relating to the integrity or security of computer networks or systems; (ii) information traditionally recognized as trade secrets; (iii) all data and information about a party’s customers (current, former or prospective) and Employees and Advisors (as such terms are defined below); (iv) the terms and conditions of this Agreement (including, without limitation, the Disclosure Purpose); and (v) all copies of any of the foregoing or any analyses, studies or reports that contain, are based on, or reflect any of the foregoing. 1.2. The confidentiality obligations set forth under this Agreement do not extend to Excluded Information. For the purposes of this Agreement, “Excluded Information” means information that the Receiving Party can demonstrate (i) was known to the Receiving Party without restriction prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (ii) became publicly available through no act or omission of the Receiving Party; (iii) is received from a third party; or (iv) was developed at any time by the Receiving Party independent of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "without breach of any obligation owed to the Disclosing Party" from section 1.2(iii), it creates a scenario where the Receiving Party could potentially use information from a third party even if that third party breached its own NDA with the Disclosing Party. This undermines the entire purpose of the NDA, especially when section 2.2 still refers to employees/advisors needing to be bound by confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, duplicate, transmit or otherwise disseminate in any manner the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s officers, directors and employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s agents, contractors and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal advisors, auditors and accountants) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information shall have a need to know and shall be bound by professional duty or in writing (in advance) to confidentiality MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT No Reservations Confidential Page 2 of 3 No Reservations (Mutual) NDA v01 and non-use obligations at least as protective of such information as this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations, and upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party shall promptly provide documentary proof of its compliance with the immediately preceding sentence.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 1.2(iii) omits the requirement that the third party providing information to the Receiving Party must not be in breach of any obligation to the Disclosing Party. This directly contradicts section 2.2, which mandates that the Receiving Party's Employees and Advisors must be bound by confidentiality obligations at least as protective as those in the agreement. The omission allows the Receiving Party to use confidential information obtained from a third party who violated their own NDA with the Disclosing Party, which is incompatible with the intent of section 2.2 to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information via restrictions on the receiving party and its agents.

perturbation.original_text: 2. RESTRICTED USE 2.1. The Receiving Party must keep Confidential Information under its control, establish and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard Confidential Information and shall treat the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature but, in any event, with no less than a reasonable degree of care. The Receiving Party shall only use the Confidential Information for the Disclosure Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. RESTRICTED USE 2.1. The Receiving Party must keep Confidential Information under its control and shall treat the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature but, in any event, with no less than a reasonable degree of care. The Receiving Party shall only use the Confidential Information for the Disclosure Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "establish and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard Confidential Information", the contract loses its explicit requirement for the Receiving Party to actively protect the confidential information. This omission weakens the confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 2.1 and creates an in-text contradiction with the confidentiality expectations described in section 5.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. UNAUTHORIZED DISCLOSURE OR USE 5.1. Each party agrees to advise the other party promptly in writing of any unauthorized misappropriation, disclosure or use by any person of the other party’s Confidential Information which may come to its attention and to take all steps reasonably requested by the disclosing party to limit, stop or otherwise remedy such misappropriation, disclosure or use.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 2.1 explicitly requires the Receiving Party to 'establish and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard Confidential Information.' The changed text omits this phrase, weakening the security obligations. This creates a contradiction with Section 5.1, which requires parties to 'take all steps reasonably requested by the disclosing party to limit, stop or otherwise remedy such misappropriation, disclosure or use' of Confidential Information. The changed text's lack of explicit security measures contradicts the expectation that the Receiving Party should proactively safeguard the information, as implied by the steps required in Section 5.1 to remedy unauthorized use.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, duplicate, transmit or otherwise disseminate in any manner the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s officers, directors and employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s agents, contractors and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal advisors, auditors and accountants) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information shall have a need to know and shall be bound by professional duty or in writing (in advance) to confidentiality MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT No Reservations Confidential Page 2 of 3 No Reservations (Mutual) NDA v01 and non-use obligations at least as protective of such information as this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations, and upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party shall promptly provide documentary proof of its compliance with the immediately preceding sentence.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, duplicate, transmit or otherwise disseminate in any manner the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, except to the Receiving Party’s officers, directors and employees (collectively “Employees”), and the Receiving Party’s agents, contractors and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal advisors, auditors and accountants) retained by the Receiving Party (collectively “Advisors”). All such persons receiving Confidential Information shall have a need to know and may be bound by professional duty or in writing (in advance) to confidentiality MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT No Reservations Confidential Page 2 of 3 No Reservations (Mutual) NDA v01 and non-use obligations at least as protective of such information as this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be liable to the Disclosing Party in the event that any of its Employees or Advisors breach such obligations, and upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party shall promptly provide documentary proof of its compliance with the immediately preceding sentence.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be bound" to "may be bound" regarding confidentiality obligations for employees and advisors fundamentally weakens the NDA. While the receiving party is liable for breaches, the lack of a definite requirement for these parties to be bound creates uncertainty. This directly contradicts the overarching intention of the agreement as stated in Recital B: 'EACH PARTY HAS AGREED THAT DISCLOSURES OF INFORMATION BETWEEN THE PARTIES SHALL BE SUBJECT TO THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THIS AGREEMENT.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: RECITALS A. EACH PARTY ENVISAGES PROVIDING CERTAIN INFORMATION TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR THE FOLLOWING DISCLOSURE PURPOSE(S): A POTENTIAL BUSINESS RELATIONSHIP; B. EACH PARTY HAS AGREED THAT DISCLOSURES OF INFORMATION BETWEEN THE PARTIES SHALL BE SUBJECT TO THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall be bound" to "may be bound" weakens the confidentiality obligations of the Receiving Party's Employees and Advisors. This directly contradicts Recital B, which states that disclosures of information between the parties "SHALL BE SUBJECT TO THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THIS AGREEMENT." The original text ensured that all recipients of confidential information were bound by confidentiality obligations, aligning with the agreement's overarching purpose. The changed text introduces uncertainty and allows for the possibility that some recipients may not be bound, creating a clear inconsistency and in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, CURRENT OR FUTURE DEVELOPMENTS 3.1. As between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, Confidential Information and all applicable intellectual property rights embodied in the Confidential Information shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any rights to the Receiving Party or any of its Employees or Advisors, by license or otherwise, to any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, except to the extent as expressly stated in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, CURRENT OR FUTURE DEVELOPMENTS 3.1. As between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, Confidential Information and all applicable intellectual property rights embodied in the Confidential Information shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any rights to the Receiving Party or any of its Employees or Advisors, by license or otherwise, to any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'except to the extent as expressly stated in this Agreement' from section 3.1 creates a contradiction. Without this caveat, the statement implies that under no circumstances does the receiving party gain any rights to the confidential information. However, Section 6.4 allows for the retention of copies under specific circumstances, creating a right to use the information, which directly conflicts with the modified section 3.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.4. Notwithstanding Section 6.3, the Receiving Party may retain a reasonable number of copies of the Confidential Information (and any materials embedding the same) (“Retained Copies”) for the sole purpose of (i) satisfying any legal or regulatory requirements regarding record and data retention that the Receiving Party is obligated to comply with; (ii) enforcing this Agreement; and/or (iii) archiving consistent with good business practices and the Receiving Party’s internal policies. For the avoidance of doubt, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use as set forth in this Agreement shall continue in respect of such Retained Copies as per the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 3.1 explicitly stated that the Receiving Party might gain rights to the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information 'except to the extent as expressly stated in this Agreement.' However, the changed text removes this exception. Section 6.4 allows the Receiving Party to retain copies of confidential information, which implies a right to use and possess that information, directly contradicting the modified Section 3.1 that now states the Receiving Party gains no rights under any circumstances, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement the Receiving Party's right to use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information ceases and the Receiving Party must, upon the Disclosing Party’s request and at the Receiving Party’s expense, promptly return to the Disclosing Party or permanently destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its power, possession or control. Having acknowledged that it, particularly within a networked environment, the return and/or destruction of electronically stored information may be impossible, extremely or otherwise unduly difficult or costly, the immediately preceding sentence is not intended to, and does not obligate the Receiving Party to make extra-ordinary efforts to destroy electronically stored copies of the Confidential Information, provided that, in relation to any such copy that is not destroyed the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use shall continue as per the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement the Receiving Party's right to use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information ceases and the Receiving Party must, upon the Disclosing Party’s request and at the Receiving Party’s expense, promptly return to the Disclosing Party or permanently destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its power, possession or control. Having acknowledged that it, particularly within a networked environment, the return and/or destruction of electronically stored information may be impossible, extremely or otherwise unduly difficult or costly, the immediately preceding sentence is not intended to, and does not obligate the Receiving Party to make extra-ordinary efforts to destroy electronically stored copies of the Confidential Information, provided that, in relation to any such copy that is not destroyed the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use shall be considered void.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall continue as per the provisions of this Agreement." to "shall be considered void", directly contradicts with section 6.4 'For the avoidance of doubt, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use as set forth in this Agreement shall continue in respect of such Retained Copies as per the provisions of this Agreement.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.4. Notwithstanding Section 6.3, the Receiving Party may retain a reasonable number of copies of the Confidential Information (and any materials embedding the same) (“Retained Copies”) for the sole purpose of (i) satisfying any legal or regulatory requirements regarding record and data retention that the Receiving Party is obligated to comply with; (ii) enforcing this Agreement; and/or (iii) archiving consistent with good business practices and the Receiving Party’s internal policies. For the avoidance of doubt, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use as set forth in this Agreement shall continue in respect of such Retained Copies as per the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the Receiving Party's obligations of confidentiality and restricted use continue as per the agreement if electronically stored copies of confidential information are not destroyed. The changed text explicitly states that these obligations are considered void in the same situation. This directly contradicts section 6.4, which states, 'For the avoidance of doubt, the Receiving Party’s obligations of confidentiality and restricted use as set forth in this Agreement shall continue in respect of such Retained Copies as per the provisions of this Agreement.' The change introduces a clear conflict regarding whether confidentiality obligations persist for retained copies, leading to a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.5. Waiver. A provision of or a right created under this Agreement may not be (i) waived except in a writing executed by the party granting the waiver; or (ii) varied except in a writing duly executed by the parties. No waiver shall be deemed a waiver of any preceding or succeeding breach or right in the same or in any other provision hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.5. Waiver. A provision of or a right created under this Agreement may not be (i) waived except in a writing executed by the party granting the waiver; or (ii) varied. No waiver shall be deemed a waiver of any preceding or succeeding breach or right in the same or in any other provision hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "except in a writing duly executed by the parties." from section 7.5, it removes the method on which a change on any provision of the contract is supposed to be made, as well as it directly contradicts with section 7.2 which is talking about the entirety of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.2. Entire Agreement. Save as expressly stated herein, this Agreement represents the entire understanding between the parties relating to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all previous agreements, understandings or commitments between the parties whether oral or written with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that any variation to the agreement requires a written document executed by all parties. The changed text removes this requirement, creating a direct conflict with section 7.2 (Entire Agreement), which emphasizes that the written agreement represents the complete understanding. By removing the requirement for written agreement from 7.5, it now implies changes can be made without written consent, thus contradicting 7.2's insistence on the written agreement's completeness. Therefore, the change introduces a strong in-text contradiction as it removes an essential constraint on how the agreement can be altered, making the contract unenforceable.
